metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

evidence: No `## Acceptance Criteria` section with `- [ ]` items found.
findings.finding: The ticket has no `## Acceptance Criteria` checklist. A plan cannot be reviewed for completion without testable, checkable criteria.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an `## Acceptance Criteria` section with `- [ ]` checklist items, one per observable, in-session-verifiable outcome.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in S4 AC2 whether 'single-sourced under adapters/jira/' means (a) the logic is only called via the Backend port (call-site neutrality), or (b) the implementation bytes exist only in adapters/jira/ files — and adjust the grep-verification wording to match what is actually achievable before S5.
evidence: S4 AC2: 'ADF size-fitting, lossy status/parent mapping, and the duplicated value-maps/link constants live only under adapters/jira/ (single-sourced) — verified by grep for those constructs returning hits only under adapters/jira/.'
S4 Scope: 'Out of scope: physically moving acli*/adf/outbound_fields/comment_limits and the loader/patch-string migration (S5).'
S4 Context: 'No files are physically relocated here (that is S5); this story changes who calls what, not where files live.'
container pairing: parent + 6673-7636-a116-4f90/94f9-a9a0-7475-4e75/ce4d-c3ca-5412-4798/dfb9-d2d1-455d-48d9/ef33-eaf6-a545-4bfb
findings.finding: S4 AC2's grep-based verification that ADF-sizing/lossy-status/value-maps 'live only under adapters/jira/' is ambiguous before S5: the root files (adf.py, outbound_fields.py) still physically exist after S4 and may still contain the original implementations, making the grep return hits at both root and adapters/jira/ paths until S5 completes the move.
scenarios: After S4, the logic is in adapters/jira/jira_fields.py AND the original code still exists in adf.py/outbound_fields.py at the root (not yet moved/deleted). grep finds constructs in both places, failing AC2's 'only under adapters/jira/' assertion.

If S4 deletes the logic from root files (while keeping the files in place), those files become hollow stubs — which may contradict S5's assumption that it is doing a 'pure relocation' of intact modules.
findings.suggested_fix: Reword S4 AC2 to: 'ADF size-fitting, lossy status/parent mapping, and the duplicated value-maps/link constants are only invoked via the Backend port in core modules (grep for direct calls returns zero hits in the 10 sites); the Jira-specific implementations reside in adapters/jira/ files. Physical de-duplication of root-file copies is completed in S5.' This separates the call-site neutrality (verifiable at S4) from the physical single-sourcing (verifiable at S5).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `inbound_fields.py` and `outbound_comments.py` to S5's file_impact list and extend the structural gate to cover `lazy_load(…, 'adf.py')` and `lazy_load(…, 'comment_limits.py')` filename-string references (not just dotted-import paths), so post-move breakage in these two non-moved callers is caught before the suite runs.
evidence: inbound_fields.py:36: `adf_path = Path(__file__).parent / 'adf.py'` — hardcoded root-relative path, breaks after adf.py moves to adapters/jira/
outbound_comments.py:84,100: `lazy_load(_ADF_KEY, 'adf.py')` and `lazy_load(_COMMENT_LIMITS_KEY, 'comment_limits.py')` — both resolve via _PACKAGE_DIR (the root), break after both files move
S5 file_impact lists _loader.py, __main__.py, adapters/jira/acli.py, tests/reconciler/conftest.py — neither inbound_fields.py nor outbound_comments.py appear

S5 structural gate: 'grep finds zero references to the old root paths of the moved modules in src/ and tests/' — a grep for dotted-import strings will miss `lazy_load(key, 'adf.py')` and `Path(__file__).parent / 'adf.py'` usages since those are filename strings, not module import paths
findings.finding: After S5 physically moves adf.py and comment_limits.py into adapters/jira/, two non-moved root modules (inbound_fields.py and outbound_comments.py) will have broken hardcoded sibling-relative filename references that are NOT in S5's file_impact list and will not be caught by the plan's 'grep for old module paths' structural gate (which targets dotted-import strings, not lazy_load filename arguments).
findings.location: S5 (dfb9-d2d1-455d-48d9) — Edit-set / Scope / Acceptance Criteria; also src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/inbound_fields.py:36, outbound_comments.py:84,100
scenarios: At S5 landing, inbound_fields._load_adf() raises FileNotFoundError because adf.py no longer exists at the root package dir
At S5 landing, outbound_comments._load_adf() and _load_comment_limits() both raise FileNotFoundError for the same reason
The structural gate (grep for old dotted-module paths) passes GREEN while both modules are broken because filename-string references evade the grep pattern
findings.suggested_fix: In S5's edit-set, explicitly enumerate all callers of `lazy_load(key, 'adf.py')` and `lazy_load(key, 'comment_limits.py')` and `Path(__file__).parent / 'adf.py'` across src/ (not just the moved files), update each to resolve under `adapters/jira/` instead of the package root, and add those files to file_impact. Extend the structural gate to: `grep -rn "'adf.py'\|'comment_limits.py'" src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/ | grep -v adapters/jira/` expecting zero hits after the move.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the loader extension mechanism in S5's Approach — e.g. a `lazy_load_adapter(key, filename, subdir)` overload, or a `_KNOWN_ADAPTER_FILES: frozenset` routing table inside `lazy_load`, or per-call callers passing explicit base dirs — so the implementer has a single unambiguous design to execute.
evidence: _loader.py:39: `_PACKAGE_DIR = Path(__file__).parent` is a module-level constant used by every lazy_load call across ~30 root modules
S5 Approach: 'Move the files, update the loader so location-pinned lookups resolve in adapters/jira/' — no mechanism stated for how the loader distinguishes root filenames from adapter filenames
S5 file_impact names _loader.py and __main__.py but gives only 'Teach the file-location loader the adapters/jira dir for location-pinned modules' as the reason, with no design detail
findings.finding: S5's 'teach the file-location loader the new dir' mechanism is unspecified at the level needed to avoid breaking the ~30 other root modules that also use lazy_load: a naive change to _PACKAGE_DIR breaks all root-resident callers, yet the plan gives no implementation sketch of how the loader will serve both root and adapters/jira/ paths simultaneously.
findings.location: S5 (dfb9-d2d1-455d-48d9) — Approach section; src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_loader.py:39 (`_PACKAGE_DIR = Path(__file__).parent`)
scenarios: Implementer changes _PACKAGE_DIR to adapters/jira/ — all root lazy_load calls (mode.py, config.py, mutation.py, etc.) break immediately
Implementer adds a parallel _ADAPTER_JIRA_DIR constant but some callers of lazy_load don't know which directory to use — requires per-caller changes that the plan hasn't enumerated
A per-filename routing table in lazy_load works but must be kept in sync with the moved-file list — an undocumented invariant

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the decomposition (S4 or S5) at which story landing the neutrality gate's two arms (`adapters.jira.*` direct imports AND root vendor module direct imports) are each expected to be non-vacuously enforced, and confirm the gate is re-run/updated as part of S5.
evidence: SC3: 'No backend-neutral core module imports `adapters.jira.*` or the root vendor modules directly — enforced by a neutrality gate (S4)'
S4 decomposition: 'Changes callers, not file locations'
S5 decomposition: 'physically relocate `acli*`/`adf`/`outbound_fields`/`comment_limits` into `adapters/jira/`'
findings.finding: The neutrality-gate success criterion (SC3) is attributed to S4, but the full predicate it asserts — 'no backend-neutral core module imports `adapters.jira.*`' — cannot be true until S5 physically moves files into `adapters/jira/`. Before S5 those modules still live at their root paths, so the `adapters.jira.*` arm of the gate is vacuously satisfied at S4 landing and the gate's full meaning only materialises post-S5. Attributing enforcement to S4 may mislead the implementer about when the gate is complete.
findings.location: Success Criteria §3 (S4 neutrality gate) vs. Child decomposition S4/S5 boundary
scenarios: Implementer writes the neutrality gate in S4 to check `adapters.jira.*` imports; the gate trivially passes (nothing lives there yet); S5 moves files but nobody updates or re-validates the gate, so the constraint is never meaningfully verified.
findings.suggested_fix: Split the gate description: S4 enforces 'no root vendor module direct imports from core'; S5 extends/re-validates the gate to also enforce 'no `adapters.jira.*` direct imports from core'. Update the SC wording to reflect both checkpoints.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate all ~5 inline AcliClient/_load_acli() call sites explicitly by file name in S3 scope/file_impact; ensure the AC3 grep covers the full set, not just the named subset.
evidence: S3 Context: 'The reconciler constructs its Jira client inline at ~5 sites (each building an AcliClient / calling the _load_acli() dynamic loader).'
S3 file_impact: lists only fetcher.py, applier.py, run_differs.py (3 sites) plus config.py, _backend_registry.py, adapters/jira/backend.py.

S3 AC3: 'All inline AcliClient constructions / _load_acli() calls at the rewired sites are replaced by select_backend(config) — verified by grep showing no remaining inline AcliClient( construction at those sites.'
S5 physically moves acli* with no shims; any site still importing the old path after S3 would break at S5 landing.
container pairing: parent + 6673-7636-a116-4f90/94f9-a9a0-7475-4e75/ce4d-c3ca-5412-4798/dfb9-d2d1-455d-48d9/ef33-eaf6-a545-4bfb
findings.finding: S3 file_impact names only 3 construction sites (fetcher.py, applier.py, run_differs.py) but the text says '~5 inline AcliClient/_load_acli() constructions'; the 2 unspecified remaining callers are a consumer-enumeration gap that, if missed, would be broken by S5's physical move of acli*.
findings.location: child 94f9-a9a0-7475-4e75 (file_impact vs. scope text)
scenarios: A 4th or 5th _load_acli() caller exists (not in S3 file_impact); it survives S3 unrewired; S5 moves acli*, breaking that caller with an ImportError only caught by S5's full suite run.
The AC3 grep is scoped to 'those sites' — if the site list is incomplete, the grep does not catch the stray caller.
findings.suggested_fix: Before S3 implementation, run grep -r 'AcliClient\|_load_acli' src/ to produce the definitive list and add each file to S3 file_impact and the AC3 verification scope.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the plan title (or add a one-line subtitle) to reflect the full S1–S5 scope — e.g. 'Phase 2: pin Backend interface, wire registry, and relocate acli*/adf/outbound_fields into adapters/jira/' — so the headline matches the body's actual work.
evidence: Title: 'Reconciler vendor-adapter seam Phase 2: relocate acli* + adf/outbound_fields into adapters/jira/'
S2: 'define the Backend Protocols + RemoteRef + a backend-agnostic contract suite + a thin JiraBackend'
S3: 'add reconciler.backend config + registry + select_backend(); route the ~5 inline AcliClient/_load_acli() constructions through it'
S4: 'rewire the 10 differ/apply leak sites through the Backend port; move ADF-sizing + lossy-status/parent + duplicated maps/link-constants into adapters/jira/'
The body opening correctly summarizes full scope ('pins a backend interface, routes the core through it, and completes the physical migration') but the title does not.
findings.finding: The plan title ('relocate acli* + adf/outbound_fields into adapters/jira/') describes only the S5 physical-move step, but the body specifies four additional stories (S1: ADR docs, S2: Backend Protocols + RemoteRef + contract suite, S3: config/registry/select_backend, S4: rewiring caller sites) that together represent substantial architectural work — not a relocation. A reviewer approving 'a file relocation' would be surprised by the scope of interface definition and core rewiring introduced in S2–S4.
findings.location: Plan title and Child decomposition section
scenarios: A plan approver or epic-tracker reading only the title authorizes a file-relocation task but receives a protocol-definition + core-rewiring epic — scope surprise at review or sprint-planning time.
findings.suggested_fix: Retitle to: 'Reconciler vendor-adapter seam Phase 2: pin Backend Protocols + registry + relocate acli*/adf/outbound_fields into adapters/jira/'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `_attestation.py` to S3's file_impact list (or explicitly scope it out with a rationale), and reconcile the '~5 inline constructions' count with the actual 6 found by grep so implementers have an accurate enumeration.
evidence: _attestation.py:82: `return mod.AcliClient(jira_url=settings.url, user=settings.user, api_token=settings.api_token, jira_project=settings.project)` — a sixth inline AcliClient construction
S3 file_impact lists fetcher.py, applier.py, run_differs.py — _attestation.py is absent
S3 AC: 'grep showing no remaining inline AcliClient( construction at those sites' — 'those sites' is qualified, but the count of ~5 does not match the 6 actual construction sites across the codebase

S4 neutrality gate: 'None of the 10 differ/apply sites import adapters.jira.* or root vendor modules directly' — _attestation.py is not a differ/apply site so this gate wouldn't fire; but leaving it unconverted makes the 'all AcliClient construction routed through select_backend' story claim misleading
findings.finding: _attestation.py's build_acli_client_from_env() contains an inline AcliClient construction site (line 82) that is not in S3's file_impact list and not mentioned in the '~5 inline AcliClient/`_load_acli()` constructions' scope; after S3 it would still construct AcliClient directly, and after S4/S5 the neutrality gate or grep could flag it unexpectedly depending on interpretation.
findings.location: S3 (94f9-a9a0-7475-4e75) — Scope, file_impact, Acceptance Criteria grep; src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_attestation.py:82
scenarios: Implementer follows the plan literally and misses _attestation.py; the attestation band path continues constructing JiraBackend directly even when a non-Jira backend is selected
A later operator sets config.reconciler.backend='github' and the attestation path silently still builds a JiraBackend

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name or enumerate the opt-in capability Protocols in the Design section (or explicitly state 'none are required for this epic; capability Protocols are deferred to be74') so an executor does not invent them ad hoc.
evidence: 'opt-in capability Protocols' appears once with no names, no list, no examples, and no AC line in Success Criteria referencing them
S2 AC says 'Backend Protocols, RemoteRef, and a contract suite' but does not mention capability Protocols, so their scope is entirely undefined
findings.finding: The 'opt-in capability Protocols' referenced in the Design section are neither named nor given acceptance criteria, leaving an executor who implements S2 to decide unilaterally what capability Protocols exist, what they expose, and how many there are.
findings.location: Design section: 'Five role Protocols … behind one Backend facade, + opt-in capability Protocols.'
scenarios: An executor implementing S2 must invent the capability Protocol set; two different executors could produce entirely different interfaces, making future review inconsistent.
If the first second-backend (be74) needs a specific capability Protocol (e.g., bulk-update, attachment upload), its absence here means be74 discovers the gap only at implementation time.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a parenthetical or sub-bullet: 'opt-in capability Protocols (none required in Phase 2; first candidates — e.g. BulkEnact, AttachmentSupport — deferred to be74)' to make the scope explicit without blocking execution.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the neutrality gate's implementation form in the S4 description (e.g., 'an import-linter rule in .importlinter, run as part of make lint') so the executor does not have to choose the mechanism.
evidence: The phrase 'neutrality gate' appears in the success criterion without a definition, a tool name, or a pointer to existing CI infrastructure
S4 child story description ('rewire the 10 differ/apply leak sites through the Backend port') does not mention the gate's implementation either
findings.finding: The 'neutrality gate' that enforces the S4 success criterion ('No backend-neutral core module imports adapters.jira.* … enforced by a neutrality gate') is referenced by name only; its implementation form (grep-based CI step, custom flake8 plugin, import-linter rule, etc.) is not specified, leaving the executor to choose.
findings.location: Success Criteria, bullet 3: 'No backend-neutral core module imports adapters.jira.* or the root vendor modules directly — enforced by a neutrality gate (S4).'
scenarios: An executor may implement a weak gate (a one-off grep in a PR description) that is not durable, leading to regressions in later stories.
Different executors choose different tools (import-linter vs. a custom script), making the gate non-reproducible across environments.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to S4: 'The neutrality gate is implemented as an import-linter (or equivalent) rule added to the repo's lint configuration, enforced in make lint, that forbids imports of adapters.jira.* or [root vendor modules] from the rebar_reconciler package.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `FakeBackend` (a minimal test-only second-backend implementation used to validate the contract suite) explicitly to the S2 story decomposition description so the deliverable is covered.
evidence: SC1: 'a contract suite passing against both `JiraBackend` and a test-only `FakeBackend` exist (S2)'
S2 decomposition: 'define the `Backend` Protocols + `RemoteRef` + a backend-agnostic contract suite + a thin `JiraBackend` (zero behavior change). No core rewiring.'
FakeBackend is absent from the S2 bullet entirely
findings.finding: `FakeBackend` is required by Success Criterion SC7 ('a contract suite passing against both `JiraBackend` and a test-only `FakeBackend`') but is not named or described as a deliverable in the S2 (or any) story decomposition. The decomposition for S2 names only 'a backend-agnostic contract suite + a thin `JiraBackend`', leaving FakeBackend as an uncovered deliverable.
findings.location: Success Criteria §1 (S2) vs. Child decomposition S2
scenarios: Implementer treats S2 as complete once JiraBackend + contract suite exist, ships without FakeBackend, and SC7 (be74 unblocked) remains unverifiable.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the S2 bullet: '…and a minimal `FakeBackend` (test-only) that passes the contract suite, proving the seam admits a second backend without core edits.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Describe the neutrality gate as an explicit deliverable in the S4 story (e.g., a CI import-check script, a custom lint rule, or a test asserting no forbidden imports), so the criterion has a concrete verify step.
evidence: SC3: 'enforced by a neutrality gate (S4)'

S4 decomposition: 'rewire the 10 differ/apply leak sites through the `Backend` port; move ADF-sizing + lossy-status/parent + duplicated maps/link-constants into `adapters/jira/`. Changes callers, not file locations.'
No mention of 'neutrality gate', import-checker, CI rule, or linter in S4 or anywhere in the decomposition
findings.finding: The 'neutrality gate' is named as an enforcement mechanism in SC3 but has no corresponding described deliverable or implementation task in any story. S4's decomposition bullet describes rewiring call sites and moving constants but does not mention creating or running a neutrality gate, leaving the criterion's verify step referencing an artefact with no described implementation.
findings.location: Success Criteria §3 vs. Child decomposition S4
scenarios: Implementer rewires call sites but never implements the gate; SC3 is manually spot-checked at review time and later silently broken by a new import.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the S4 bullet: 'Implement a neutrality gate (import-checker test or CI script) that asserts no file under `src/rebar/_engine/` imports `adapters.jira.*` or the pre-move root vendor modules directly; gate runs in `make lint` or `make test`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan for S5's 'no shims' physical relocation (e.g., 'revert the S5 commit atomically via git revert; old paths are fully removed only after CI is green on the feature branch') so recovery from a partial migration is unambiguous.
evidence: 'physically relocate acli*/adf/outbound_fields/comment_limits into adapters/jira/ … and migrate the ~29 test patch strings atomically (no shims)'
'no re-export shims' is the explicit choice but no rollback path is described
Success Criteria: 'all ~29 test patch strings are migrated with no re-export shims' — hard cut with no stated back-out
findings.finding: S5's 'no re-export shims' physical relocation is an explicitly justified breaking change but lacks a stated rollback or back-out plan. T4 requires the remedy to include an explicit rollback/back-out path — acknowledgement alone is insufficient. If the atomic 29-patch-string migration is partially applied or causes unexpected failures, there is no documented recovery strategy (e.g., feature-branch revert, interim shim layer, or phased cut-over).
findings.location: Child decomposition §S5; Success Criteria bullet 4
scenarios: S5 migration partially lands before a blocking CI failure; no shims means callers are broken with no fast-forward recovery path
A downstream internal consumer (outside the test suite) imports from the old path and breaks with no bridge available
findings.suggested_fix: Add a single sentence under S5 or in Success Criteria: 'Back-out: S5 is landed as a single atomic commit; if CI does not go green within N hours the commit is reverted in full — no partial states are accepted. Pre-migration: confirm zero external-repo imports of the old paths via a grep gate before landing.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Identify and update the user-facing config reference (README config table, schema file, or equivalent) for the new `config.reconciler.backend` key, including its default value (`"jira"`), accepted values, and where to add new backend identifiers.
evidence: 'add reconciler.backend config + registry + select_backend(); route the ~5 inline AcliClient/_load_acli() constructions through it'
No mention of updating a config reference doc, README config table, or schema changelog for the new key
findings.finding: The new `config.reconciler.backend` configuration key introduced in S3 is a new user-visible config surface but the plan names no doc update target (README, configuration reference, or schema changelog) for it, risking an undocumented config knob.
findings.location: Child decomposition §S3; Success Criteria bullet 2
scenarios: An operator upgrading rebar cannot discover the new backend key without reading source code
The config schema doc (if one exists) lists no reconciler.backend entry, causing confusion about valid values and defaults

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a step in S5 (or S1) to audit `docs/` and ADR 0035 for references to old module paths (`acli`, `adf`, `outbound_fields`, `comment_limits` at their pre-migration locations) and update them as part of the landing commit.
evidence: 'physically relocate acli*/adf/outbound_fields/comment_limits into adapters/jira/' — old paths are deleted, not shimmed
No mention of a doc-reference audit or grep for old import paths in docs/
ADR 0035 prose written before S5 may name the old file locations
findings.finding: S5 physically renames/moves module files (`acli*`, `adf`, `outbound_fields`, `comment_limits`) but the plan does not call out an audit of existing documentation references (developer guides, existing ADR prose, or cross-references within `docs/`) that may point to the old module paths, risking stranded references post-migration.
findings.location: Child decomposition §S5
scenarios: ADR 0035 §(d) or §(c) references the old file paths by name; after S5 those references are stale
A contributor guide or CONTRIBUTING.md references the old module layout and leads new contributors to the wrong location

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] S1's AC (and ADR §(d)) must require that each role Protocol's method signatures (parameter names, types, return types) are explicitly tabulated — not just named — so S2's contract suite can be authored RED-first against a pinned spec.
evidence: ADR §(d) current text reads only: 'a transport/CRUD module … outbound field mapping … inbound field extraction … sanitization/limits … identity convention' — no method signatures
S1 AC: 'names all five role Protocols, the Backend facade, RemoteRef' — does not require method signatures to be pinned
S2 Approach: 'Derive each Protocol's method signatures from the de-facto surface the core already calls' — informal derivation, not a pinned spec
grep for 'TicketTransport', 'OutboundMapper', 'InboundMapper', 'FieldSanitizer', 'IdentityConvention' returns zero hits in repo — none of these exist yet and no plan artifact pins their signatures
findings.finding: The five role Protocols and three capability Protocols are referenced by name throughout S2–S5 and the contract suite, but their method signatures (parameter names, types, return types) are never pinned in any plan artifact — the ADR §(d) (the authoritative location) contains only prose descriptions, and S1's AC requires only that §(d) 'names' the Protocols, not that it specifies their signatures. S2 defers signature derivation to an informal rule ('derive from the de-facto surface the core already calls'), leaving an executor no verifiable oracle.
findings.location: Design section (Protocol list); S1 AC; S2 Approach ('Derive each Protocol's method signatures from the de-facto surface the core already calls'); docs/adr/0035-reconciler-vendor-adapter-seam.md §(d)
scenarios: Two engineers implement S2 independently and produce incompatible Protocol signatures (e.g., one uses positional args, another keyword-only), causing S3/S4 to break at integration.
The contract suite in S2 cannot be authored RED-first without knowing what methods to call on the backend_factory fixture.
FakeBackend in tests/ cannot satisfy the contract suite if method signatures are not pinned before implementation begins.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend S1's AC to: 'ADR §(d) contains a method-signature table for each of the five role Protocols and three capability Protocols (parameter names, types, return types), verified by reading the rendered table.' Add a stub Python snippet in §(d) showing each Protocol's `def` lines.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] S1 (or S2's AC) must pin the field types and valid value conventions for all three RemoteRef fields (e.g., `vendor: str`, `instance: str`, `remote_id: str`, and that `vendor='jira'` for JiraBackend) so downstream stories have an unambiguous schema to code against.
evidence: Plan Design: 'One new type RemoteRef{vendor, instance, remote_id} replaces the hardcoded "jira" literal' — no field types given
S2 description: 'RemoteRef{vendor, instance, remote_id} dataclass' — still no types
S4 AC: 'no hardcoded "jira" literal' — but the valid value for vendor is not stated anywhere
ADR §(d) current text: no mention of RemoteRef field types
findings.finding: RemoteRef{vendor, instance, remote_id} is referenced in the Design, S2, S3, S4, and the success criteria, but the field types and valid value conventions (e.g., is `vendor` the string 'jira'? a Python enum? a class constant? what type is `remote_id` — str? int?) are never specified anywhere in the plan. S4 explicitly replaces the hardcoded `"jira"` literal with `RemoteRef`, but what value to put in `vendor` for a JiraBackend is undefined.
findings.location: Design section ('One new type RemoteRef{vendor, instance, remote_id}'); S2 file_impact (_backend.py); S4 AC ('apply_inbound_records.py uses RemoteRef/IdentityConvention with no hardcoded "jira" literal')
scenarios: S4 implementor puts `vendor="jira"` (string) in RemoteRef; S2 implementor defined vendor as an Enum — type mismatch at integration.
The neutrality grep gate in S4 checks for the `"jira"` string literal but passes even if the wrong type is used for RemoteRef.vendor.
FakeBackend in contract suite stamps RemoteRef with a different vendor convention than JiraBackend, breaking the IdentityConvention round-trip test.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to S1 AC: 'ADR §(d) includes a RemoteRef dataclass stub with annotated field types and a note that vendor=\"jira\" for JiraBackend.' Alternatively, include it in S2's file_impact description for _backend.py.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] S1 (or S2's description/AC) must explicitly state Backend's composition rule: whether it is a flat Protocol aggregating all role-Protocol methods, a structural Protocol of named sub-protocol attributes, or another pattern — so 'JiraBackend implements Backend' is checkable by the type system.
evidence: Plan: 'Five role Protocols (TicketTransport, OutboundMapper, InboundMapper, FieldSanitizer, IdentityConvention) behind one Backend facade' — the word 'behind' is ambiguous (aggregation? inheritance? delegation?)
S2 AC: 'JiraBackend implements Backend; the contract suite passes against it' — 'implements' implies a structural check but the check's mechanism is undefined
ADR §(d): no Backend composition rule present in the current file
findings.finding: The `Backend` facade's composition rule is referenced throughout S2–S5 ('JiraBackend implements Backend', 'select_backend returns a Backend', 'FakeBackend passes the contract suite') but is never defined: the plan does not state whether Backend is a structural Protocol that role-Protocol methods are collected on, a dataclass of Protocol-typed fields, an ABC, or a union type. Without this, 'implements Backend' has no verifiable meaning.
findings.location: Design section ('Five role Protocols … behind one Backend facade'); S2 AC ('JiraBackend implements Backend'); S3 AC ('select_backend returns a Backend')
scenarios: One implementor makes Backend a Protocol with all role-Protocol methods flattened onto it; another makes it a dataclass with typed sub-protocol fields — incompatible, both pass Python's duck-typing but the contract suite cannot be written without knowing which.
mypy/pyright check 'JiraBackend implements Backend' differently depending on whether Backend is a Protocol or ABC.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] S2 must specify FakeBackend's minimal behavioral requirements (e.g., in-memory store, round-trip identity for outbound→inbound, idempotent sanitizer, RemoteRef stamp/find) so the contract suite can be written as a real oracle rather than a structural smoke test.
evidence: S2 Testing: 'FakeBackend is test-only (lives under tests/, never shipped)' — only placement is specified, not behavior
S2 Approach: 'FakeBackend' mentioned only as a parameterization target, never described
Epic SC: 'verified by the proof-of-seam FakeBackend passing the contract suite with no core edit' — but what FakeBackend must do to pass is undefined
Absence: no plan section or ADR describes FakeBackend's return values, state model, or invariants
findings.finding: FakeBackend is referenced in S2 as satisfying the full contract suite alongside JiraBackend, but its minimal behavioral specification is entirely absent: there is no description of what FakeBackend returns for each Protocol method, how it simulates transport, or what invariants it upholds. An executor must invent its behavior from scratch with no oracle.
findings.location: S2 Testing section ('an in-memory FakeBackend'); S2 AC ('FakeBackend passing the contract suite with no core edit'); Epic Success Criteria ('verified by the proof-of-seam FakeBackend passing the contract suite')
scenarios: Implementor writes a FakeBackend that returns empty/None for all methods and the contract suite trivially passes because the suite only checks that methods are callable, not their return values — defeating the proof-of-seam.
A second-backend author referencing FakeBackend as a template implements a real backend incorrectly because the fake's behavior is arbitrary.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an 800-LOC module-size cap verification step to S2 (ce4d), S3 (94f9), and S4 (6673) acceptance criteria, not only S5.
evidence: Parent SC5: 'Every src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/*.py stays within the 800-LOC module-size cap (.github/module-size-limit.txt) throughout.'
S5 (dfb9) AC4: 'Every src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/*.py stays within the 800-LOC module-size cap … after the move — verified by the module-size gate.'
S2 (ce4d): adds _backend.py — no size gate in AC.
S3 (94f9): adds _backend_registry.py — no size gate in AC.
S4 (6673): injects ADF-sizing + lossy-status/parent + value-maps into adapters/jira/jira_fields.py — no size gate in AC.
container pairing: parent + 6673-7636-a116-4f90/94f9-a9a0-7475-4e75/ce4d-c3ca-5412-4798/dfb9-d2d1-455d-48d9/ef33-eaf6-a545-4bfb
findings.finding: Parent SC5 ('throughout') is only partially covered: S5 is the only child with an 800-LOC module-size-cap verification gate, but S2 adds _backend.py, S3 adds _backend_registry.py, and S4 injects substantial field-mapping logic into adapters/jira/jira_fields.py — none with a per-landing cap check.
findings.location: child dfb9-d2d1-455d-48d9 (sole checker); children ce4d-c3ca-5412-4798, 94f9-a9a0-7475-4e75, 6673-7636-a116-4f90 (absent gate)
scenarios: S4 moves significant logic into adapters/jira/jira_fields.py; if that file approaches 800 LOC the cap is breached mid-epic and not caught until S5.
S2 or S3 creates a new file that is already large; it is only checked after S5's unrelated move.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to each of S2, S3, S4 ACs: 'Every src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/*.py stays within the 800-LOC cap after this story lands — verified by the module-size gate.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in S2 or S3 how FakeBackend is registered in the backend registry for test-time use (e.g., expose a test-registration helper, or document that the teeth test calls register() directly before selecting).
evidence: S3 Testing: 'Teeth: point the config at a FakeBackend key and assert the pass drives the fake's transport instead of Jira — confirming selection is actually honored end-to-end.'
S2 Scope: 'FakeBackend is test-only (lives under tests/, never shipped).'
S3 describes the registry as populated by '@register decorator at import time'; there is no described API for test-time registration of a non-shipped backend.
container pairing: parent + 6673-7636-a116-4f90/94f9-a9a0-7475-4e75/ce4d-c3ca-5412-4798/dfb9-d2d1-455d-48d9/ef33-eaf6-a545-4bfb
findings.finding: S3's 'teeth' test requires FakeBackend to be registered in the production backend registry, but FakeBackend lives in tests/ (never shipped per S2) with no specified mechanism for test-time registration, creating an unresolved dependency gap at the S2/S3 boundary.
findings.location: child 94f9-a9a0-7475-4e75 (Testing section, 'Teeth' bullet); child ce4d-c3ca-5412-4798 (Scope, 'FakeBackend is test-only (lives under tests/, never shipped)')
scenarios: Test engineer implements S3 teeth test, imports FakeBackend from tests/, calls select_backend('fake') — but 'fake' is not registered in _backend_registry unless the test explicitly triggers registration; no mechanism is specified.
If the test directly manipulates the registry dict (bypassing the decorator), it couples test infrastructure to internal state and is not consistent with the decorator-registry pattern described.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to S2 (ce4d) scope: 'Expose a test-registration helper (e.g., register_for_test(key, factory)) or document that test code may call register() directly; confirm this is used by S3 teeth test.' Alternatively, describe FakeBackend as having an @register decorator that is only activated in test fixtures.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
